summary: This brief teaser post introduces Joel's 'Iceberg Secret' essay through a satirical vignette of a clueless CEO. The CEO mistakes the natural slowdown after an initial prototype phase for laziness, while himself lounging on a golf course. He contemplates firing developers to motivate the team, completely misunderstanding why software projects appear to slow down after early rapid progress. The post sets up Joel's longer essay about the disconnect between visible progress and actual progress in software development.
key_points: CEOs often misinterpret the natural pace of software development after the initial prototype phase as developer laziness
The contrast between the CEO golfing while questioning his team's work ethic highlights management hypocrisy
Early prototypes create misleading expectations about development velocity that persist throughout a project
money_quotes: Things were going so well when we started this project. For the first couple of weeks, the team cranked like crazy and got a great prototype working. But since then, things seem to have slowed to a crawl.
He chooses a Callaway Titanium Driver and sends the caddy to fetch an ice-cold lemonade. "Maybe if I fire a couple of laggards that'll light a fire under them!"
key_insight: The disconnect between executive perception and software development reality leads managers to blame developers for a slowdown that is actually the normal progression from prototype to production.
